heading: NOTE AL SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO CXXXI
card_summary: This chunk consists entirely of Campanini's endnotes (notes 91–139) to his introductory essay on Francesco Zorzi, documenting the scholarly apparatus behind claims about Zorzi's biography and his 1479 pilgrimage to the Holy Land. The notes cite cross-references between the De harmonia mundi and the Problemata, establishing Zorzi's eyewitness autopsy of sites including Beirut/Berytus, Mount Carmel, Mount Lebanon, Jerusalem, the Probatica piscina, the Holy Sepulchre, the Mount of Olives, and the Valley of Jehoshaphat. Campanini also documents Zorzi's birth date controversy (1453, 1460, or 1466 per the horoscope), his alchemical experiments mentioned in De harmonia mundi 1,7,22, and the topos of Jerusalem as the "medium mundi" — a phrase appearing in a marble inscription at the Holy Sepulchre that Zorzi saw with his own eyes, later quoted by Ezra Pound in Canto LXXXVII.
essay:
# Notes to the Introductory Essay (Notes 91–139): Zorzi's Biography and Pilgrimage Autopsy

## Overview

This chunk is composed entirely of Campanini's scholarly endnotes (numbered 91–139) to his introductory essay on Francesco Zorzi. The notes supply bibliographic references and extended Latin citations from Zorzi's own works — principally the *De harmonia mundi* (DHM) and the *Problemata* — that substantiate Campanini's narrative of Zorzi's life, specifically his 1479 pilgrimage to the Holy Land, and the way that personal experience of sacred geography saturates both his major works.

## Zorzi's Birth Date

Note 94 resolves a contested scholarly question about Zorzi's birth year. Campanini follows Maillard (1991, p. 230) in confirming 1466 as the correct date, as derived from an extant horoscope. He explicitly rejects the alternative dates proposed in earlier scholarship: 1460 (cited by multiple sources) and 1453 (the hypothesis of Vicentini 1954–1957, p. 131ff).

## The Pilgrimage and Eyewitness Authority

Notes 95–139 form the densest concentration of material, documenting the scholarly apparatus behind Campanini's account of Zorzi's 1479 pilgrimage. A remarkable feature of both the DHM and the *Problemata* is Zorzi's repeated invocation of personal autopsy — "ut oculis propriis perspeximus," "ut oculis propriis vidimus," "ut novimus et vidimus" — as an epistemological warrant for his exegetical and cosmological claims.

Note 97 is particularly striking: it reproduces a passage from the German pilgrim Ludwig von Greiffenstein's chronicle (published by Schön 1892, p. 458), recording in Middle High German the encounter with Venetian merchant galleys near Cyprus on 10 September — the same fleet on which Zorzi was travelling, though Greiffenstein does not name him:

> Hunndert und xxxii tag (10 settembre), An mittwoch nach unser frauen tag, als sy geporn ist warden, fueren wir noch neben dem lannd zuo Zypern hin und wier heten kain wint, der mit uns gewesen wär, und was uns die weyl lanck und IIII stund nach mittag sachen wier drei gale gegen uns daher gen.

<cite>Chunk 29, note 97 (citing Schön 1892, p. 458, from Ludwig von Greiffenstein's chronicle)</cite>

## Sacred Geography: Phoenicia, Lebanon, and the Levantine Coast

Several notes (98–106) document Zorzi's detailed treatment of Phoenician geography in the DHM and *Problemata*. Note 100 quotes DHM 2,7,17 on Berytus (Beirut) as the site of a Franciscan church where Latins, Maronites, and all adherents of the Roman rite gather:

> Et hucusque laudes Dei, et Christi eius in Phoenicia persolvuntur, in Beruto videlicet civitate in Phoeniciae meditullio collocata, ubi est ecclesia fratrum minorum, in quam non modo ipsi fratres, sed Maronitae, et quotquot ritum ecclesiae Romanae observant conveniunt.

<cite>Chunk 29, note 100 (DHM 2,7,17)</cite>

Notes 101–104 excerpt the *Problemata*'s treatment of Mount Lebanon as the watershed between Damascus and Berytus, the sources of the Jordan (the springs Ior and Dan), and the extent of Maronite/Chaldean Christian territory. Note 105 quotes DHM 2,7,16 on Mount Carmel and the port of Ptolemais (Acre). Note 106 cross-references *Problemata* 3,5,209 on the tradition that Jonah was disgorged by the fish at Ptolemais/Acre.

## The Jewish People and the Dimming of Christianity in Judaea

Note 109 quotes DHM 2,7,14 on the spiritual condition of the Holy Land in Zorzi's time. The passage interprets the scattering of the Jewish people as analogous to "waters poured out" (aquae effusae), with the charisms evacuated from them. The passage closes with a pointed contemporary observation about the residual "small spark" of Christianity surviving in the region, tied exegetically to Moses' oracle "vivat Reuben et non moriatur" (Deut. 33:6):

> Hinc aquae effusae bene aequiparatur populus Iudaicus, quia statim evacuata sunt omnia charismata ab eo; defecit enim cito ecclesia Ierosolymitana et ea quae erat in tota Iudaea, adeo ut nihil christianitatis ibi relictum sit, nisi tantummodo nomina, et aliqualis memoria locorum […] Vivit igitur adhuc in illa regione parvulus igniculus Christianitatis, ut habeant locum illud Mosaici oraculi, vivat Reuben, et non moriatur, et sit parvulus in numero.

<cite>Chunk 29, note 109 (DHM 2,7,14)</cite>

## Alchemy and First-Hand Experiment

Note 110 opens a brief aside on Zorzi's relationship to alchemy, citing François Secret's studies (1973, 1992). The evidence comes from DHM 1,7,22, where Zorzi records a personal experiment attempting to dissolve crystals with intense heat: "Bdelius vero idest crystallus, vel talcus nullo sunt calore resolubiles, ut clara experientia comprobavimus." This is consistent with Zorzi's broader epistemology of autopsy — empirical verification as a supplement to textual authority.

## Jerusalem as Medium Mundi

Note 121 documents one of the most resonant topoi in the chunk: the inscription "Hic est medium mundi" on a marble stone inside the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, which Zorzi reports seeing with his own eyes (*Problemata* 3,4,178). Campanini traces this topos through medieval pilgrimage literature (Reichert 2001) and notes, remarkably, that it surfaces in Ezra Pound's *Canto LXXXVII*:

> "Here Christ put a thumb on a rock / Saying: hic est medium mundi" / (That, I assure you, happened / Ego scriptor cantilenae).

<cite>Chunk 29, note 121 (citing Problemata 3,4,178 and Ezra Pound, Canto LXXXVII)</cite>

Note 122 (*Problemata* 4,5,249) deploys the same topos Christologically: Christ chose Jerusalem for his Passion precisely because it sits at the centre of the world.

## The Holy Sepulchre and the Seven Nations

Note 124 quotes an extended passage from *Problemata* 3,5,213 on the Church of the Holy Sepulchre, identifying seven Christian nations who continuously celebrate praise there: Latins, Greeks, Indians called Abyssinians, Armenians, Georgians, Maronites (identified with Chaldeans), and Jacobites. The passage also catalogues the sites contained within the great church built by Helena, mother of Constantine: the Sepulchre itself, Calvary, the Garden of the Resurrection, the place of the Three Crosses, and the place where Jesus' body was anointed.

## The Probatica Piscina and Hebrew Etymology

Note 130 contains a notable philological excursus from *Problemata* 4,7,431, where Zorzi explains the Hebrew etymology of the Probatica (sheep pool, John 5:2). He derives *betsaidah* from *bet* (house) + *seh* (lamb/sheep), offering the Hebrew characters in the text, and interprets the pool's miraculous healing power — activated when the water was stirred — as a figura of baptism instituted by "that Lamb who took away the sins of the world."

## The Mount of Olives and the Valley of Jehoshaphat

Note 135 excerpts *Problemata* 4,6,379 on Christ weeping over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41): Zorzi locates the scene precisely on the Mount of Olives, with Jerusalem visible ahead and the Valley of Jehoshaphat — etymologized as "valley of judgment" — between the Mount and the city. This topographic precision carries eschatological weight: the valley of future judgment lay literally before Christ's eyes as he wept.

## Connections to the Larger Project

Throughout these notes, the governing concern is Zorzi's fusion of pilgrimage autopsy with cosmic exegesis. His eyewitness observations of Holy Land geography — the scent of the Promised Land detectable at sea, the pomegranate orchards near Jerusalem, the inscribed "medium mundi" stone, the palm's properties he verified personally — are not mere travel memoir. They function as empirical anchors for the harmonist cosmology of the DHM, in which the physical world bears legible signs of divine order, and direct experience (*experientia*) validates scriptural and Kabbalistic interpretation alike.
